questions.text: Can you summarize this video for me? What's the big misconception about physics it's talking about, and what are the main points it makes?
questions.why_verifiable: A good answer must cover the core arc: energy conservation is not universally true, Emmy Noether's theorem linking continuous symmetries to conservation laws (translation->momentum, rotation->angular momentum, time->energy), the expanding universe breaking time symmetry so energy is not conserved, her second theorem/local symmetries resolving the general relativity problem, and her biography (Gottingen, Nazi expulsion, Bryn Mawr).

questions.text: The video talks about light from the early universe losing energy as space expands. How much of its energy does one of those ancient photons lose by the time it reaches our telescopes, and where does that energy actually go?
questions.why_verifiable: Transcript gives exact figures and a concrete claim: a photon emitted 380,000 years after the Big Bang arrives as a microwave having lost 99.9% of its energy, and the energy 'doesn't go anywhere' because energy isn't conserved without time symmetry (expanding universe). Numbers 380,000 and 99.9% are checkable against the transcript.

questions.text: What exactly was the mistake Einstein made about energy conservation in general relativity, and how did Emmy Noether prove him wrong?
questions.why_verifiable: Transcript has concrete verifiable specifics: Einstein's proposed conservation equation (matter energy + gravitational field energy constant) contained a pseudotensor that changes between reference frames, violating general covariance; Noether showed via her second theorem that local symmetries only yield a continuity equation, which turned out to be exactly the Bianchi identities Hilbert had dismissed, the best one can do in general relativity.

answers.answer:
SUMMARY - The video (Veritasium, 'The Biggest Misconception in Physics', 27:39) argues that the biggest misconception in physics is that energy is always conserved. It shows that conservation laws are not fundamental bedrock; they are consequences of symmetries (Emmy Noether's theorems), and because our expanding universe is not symmetric in time, energy as we usually think of it is NOT conserved [16:56], [17:26].

SEGMENTS (in order):
1. Cold open - an astronaut in deep space throws a rock; instead of coasting forever per Newton's first law, over cosmic time it slows down and stops, raising 'where did the rock's energy go?' [00:02]-[00:24] (animated astronaut shown on screen at [00:00]).
2. Framing - at the turn of the 20th century energy conservation baffled Einstein; he proposed a solution, and a 'little-known unpaid mathematician' Emmy Noether proved him wrong, creating a new paradigm underlying all of particle physics [00:26]-[00:52].
3. 1915 Gottingen - Einstein gives six lectures on his emerging general relativity but can't show total energy is conserved; David Hilbert searches and finds only the Bianchi identities, which conserve energy only in a completely empty universe, so he hands the problem to his new assistant Emmy Noether [01:00]-[02:19] (animated lecture hall at [01:33]-[01:38]).
4. Noether's backstory - daughter of a math professor at Erlangen; the university refused to admit women ('would overthrow all academic order'), so in 1903 she spent a semester at Gottingen and learned symmetry-based geometry [02:21]-[02:55].
5. Symmetry explainer - a physical demo with an equilateral triangle: 3 mirror axes plus rotations by 120/240/360 degrees = six symmetries; then continuous symmetry, e.g. adding a constant to a function leaves its derivative unchanged [02:56]-[04:24] (triangle demo on screen at [02:59]-[03:33]).
6. Einstein's proposed fix - Noether becomes a symmetry expert and only the second woman in Germany with a math PhD; Einstein proposes a conservation equation: matter energy + gravitational field energy = constant [04:24]-[04:59]. On-screen (never fully read aloud): a hand writes the equation with terms like d_mu(sqrt(g) T^mu_nu + t^mu_nu) at [00:37]/[05:22 frame].
7. Why she knew it was wrong / GR recap - special relativity 1905 (on-screen: the paper 'On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies, A. Einstein, June 30, 1905' at [04:43]); Einstein's 'happiest thought' (a falling window cleaner feels weightless), the equivalence principle (rocket accelerating at 9.8 m/s^2 = standing on Earth), general covariance, and why GR must be built from tensors, which stay the same across coordinate systems [05:10]-[08:45] (on-screen at [09:41]: the field equation G_mu_nu = 8piG/c^4 T_mu_nu over a curved spacetime grid).
8. The flaw - Einstein's equation contained a pseudotensor: gravitational energy seen in one frame disappears in another; physicists were 'bending the rules of mathematics' to force conservation in [08:45]-[09:24].
9. Noether's reframe - general covariance is itself a symmetry; she asks what symmetries of the universe imply. In an empty static universe: spatial translation symmetry gives conservation of momentum, rotational symmetry gives conservation of angular momentum [09:24]-[11:43].
10. Time symmetry derivation - using the principle of least action and the Euler-Lagrange equation, a whiteboard derivation shows time-translation symmetry implies dE/dt = 0, i.e. energy conservation [11:59]-[15:35] (on-screen whiteboard 'Time Symmetry', t -> t + epsilon, L' = L + epsilon dL/dt at [13:04]-[14:06]; final on-screen line d/dt(1/2 mv^2 + V) = 0 hence dE/dt = 0 labeled 'Constant' at [15:28]).
11. Noether's first theorem - every continuous symmetry yields a conservation law; this explains the origin of all conservation laws [15:35]-[16:07].
12. Our universe breaks the symmetry - 1920s: galaxies recede (expansion); 1990s: supernova measurements show accelerating expansion; so the universe is not time-symmetric over large scales, and energy isn't conserved [16:16]-[17:05]. A CMB-era photon (emitted 380,000 years after the Big Bang) arrives as a microwave having lost 99.9% of its energy; 'it doesn't go anywhere - energy is not conserved', which also resolves the opening rock puzzle [17:05]-[17:53].
13. Why energy seems conserved - on short timescales time-translation symmetry effectively holds; only over millions of years does the expansion matter [18:24]-[18:54].
14. Noether's second theorem - GR has no global symmetries (curvature varies), but general covariance is a local symmetry; local symmetries give only continuity equations, not proper conservation laws. Water-pipe analogy: locally energy is conserved, but spacetime curvature acts like cracks between pipe sections through which energy 'leaks', attributed to gravitational-field/curvature terms. This equation is exactly the Bianchi identities Hilbert dismissed - and it is the best you can do in GR [18:54]-[22:45].
15. Legacy and recognition - one paper solved what eluded Hilbert and Einstein; an interviewee calls her two theorems 'probably the most important theorems for physics of the 20th century'; Gottingen made her a professor with a small salary from 1923 [22:51]-[23:20]. (On-screen experts include Melissa Franklin, Professor of Physics, Harvard University, identified by on-screen caption at [01:24].)
16. Nazi persecution - January 30, 1933: Jewish academics banned; a former student denounced her and she was suspended; she kept teaching from her kitchen, even a student in a Nazi stormtrooper brownshirt; she escaped to Bryn Mawr, a women's college in America, taught there until her death; Einstein's NYT obituary called her 'the most significant creative mathematical genius thus far produced since the higher education of women began' [23:20]-[24:35] (archival Nazi footage at [23:25]-[23:28]; kitchen-teaching and brownshirt scenes at [23:46]-[23:50]; Bryn Mawr building at [24:15]).
17. Modern impact + teaser - physicists adopted the symmetry mindset; electron phase (gauge) symmetry gives conservation of electric charge; in the 1960s-70s her insights led to quarks and the Higgs boson, the origin of forces and mass - 'the closest we've ever come to a theory of everything'; a second video is promised [24:37]-[25:54].
18. KiwiCo sponsor segment - Derek's kids build a 'Rapid-Fire Disc Launcher' crate; on-screen promo text 'KiwiCo.com/Veritasium, Code: Veritasium, 50% Off First Crate' [26:04]-[27:32] (on-screen overlay visible at [27:04]-[27:11]).

HOW MUCH ENERGY IS LOST: The video describes a photon of visible light emitted 380,000 years after the Big Bang that travels unimpeded through the universe and arrives at our telescopes not as visible light but as a microwave - it has lost 99.9% of its energy [17:05]-[17:22].

WHERE THE ENERGY GOES: The video's direct answer is that it 'doesn't go anywhere. Energy is not conserved' [17:24]-[17:29]. Because the universe is expanding (and that expansion is accelerating), the universe is not symmetric in time over large timescales [16:36]-[16:53], and per Noether's theorem, without time-translation symmetry there is no reason for energy to be conserved [16:54]-[17:05]. The same reasoning resolves the opening rock-in-space puzzle: the rock slows and stops in the expanding universe, and 'the energy doesn't really go anywhere. It just disappears' [17:31]-[17:44]; this violates no laws of physics because energy and momentum are only conserved when time or spatial symmetry holds [17:51]-[17:57].

NUANCE THE VIDEO ADDS LATER (reconciling two moments): At [17:24] the answer is a flat 'doesn't go anywhere', but at [22:00]-[22:19] the video says that in Noether's GR continuity equation, as energy decreases in the local-conservation term, the curvature terms increase - 'the energy that you lose from the system you're tracking, we now start attributing it to things like the gravitational field, which has changed because the whole universe is stretched.' These aren't contradictory claims but two framings the video itself uses: there is no proper global conservation law (the energy genuinely isn't conserved, [17:26], [19:57]-[20:07]), yet in the local bookkeeping of the continuity equation the loss shows up in curvature/gravitational-field terms [22:00]-[22:26]. Energy remains conserved only within any small local patch of spacetime; linking patches through curved spacetime is like cracks between pipe sections through which energy can leak [21:07]-[21:27]. The video also notes energy seems conserved in everyday life because on short timescales time symmetry effectively holds; only over millions of years does the expansion break it [18:26]-[18:54].

EINSTEIN'S MISTAKE: Struggling to show total energy was conserved in general relativity [01:14]-[01:19], Einstein proposed a new conservation equation stating that if you add the energy of matter and the energy of the gravitational field, the total remains constant - its change over time and space is zero [04:42]-[04:57]. On-screen content (not read aloud): a hand is shown writing this equation with terms like d_mu(sqrt(g) T^mu_nu + t^mu_nu) at [00:37] and again at [05:22]. When Noether saw it, she was convinced he had made a fundamental mistake because the equation 'disregards the foundational principle that general relativity is built on' [04:59]-[05:10] - general covariance, the requirement that the laws of physics take the same form in every frame of reference [07:36]-[07:40], which is why the theory must be built from tensors, objects that stay the same when you change coordinate systems [07:40]-[08:45]. The specific flaw: Einstein's energy-conservation equation contained a pseudotensor, which is not a true tensor - when you transform it between frames of reference it does not remain the same quantity, so the gravitational energy you observe in one frame completely disappears in another [08:47]-[09:08]. As an interviewed physicist puts it, people were trying to 'stamp conservation of energy into relativity by bending the rules of mathematics' [09:13]-[09:24].

HOW NOETHER PROVED HIM WRONG: Knowing the pseudotensor equation couldn't be the answer [09:24]-[09:29], she asked whether general covariance and energy conservation might simply be incompatible [09:29]-[09:38]. She recognized general covariance as a kind of symmetry - exactly her specialty [09:38]-[09:49] - and worked out what symmetries imply. Her FIRST theorem: every continuous symmetry yields a conservation law (spatial translation -> momentum, rotation -> angular momentum, time translation -> energy), demonstrated in the video via a least-action/Euler-Lagrange derivation ending in dE/dt = 0 [12:01]-[15:35], [15:35]-[16:07] (whiteboard derivation on-screen [13:04]-[15:28]). But those are symmetries of a static, empty universe [16:07]-[16:12]. In general relativity, where curvature changes from point to point, shifting/rotating/time-evolving the whole universe does not leave things the same, so those global symmetries are gone [19:03]-[19:25]; general covariance survives only as a LOCAL symmetry [19:31]-[19:54]. Her SECOND theorem proved that local symmetries do not give proper conservation laws at all - only a continuity equation that works locally [19:54]-[20:07]. In GR that continuity equation picks up curvature terms: energy is conserved in any small patch of spacetime, but linking patches through curved spacetime is like cracks between pipe sections through which energy leaks, the loss being attributed to the changing gravitational field [20:49]-[22:26]. She then realized this continuity equation was exactly equivalent to the Bianchi identities - the 'half solution' Hilbert had found and dismissed because it gave proper energy conservation only in an empty universe - and proved it was the best you could do in general relativity [22:28]-[22:45] (on-screen at [26:07]: Noether at a blackboard with the equation d_mu(X^mu_nu + t^mu_nu) = 0). In other words, she proved Einstein wrong by showing that the global energy conservation he was trying to force into the theory cannot exist in general relativity - with one paper solving the problem that had eluded both Hilbert and Einstein [22:51]-[22:58].

SUMMARY: This is a Veritasium video about Emmy Noether, symmetry, and why energy conservation is not actually a universal law of physics.

THE BIG MISCONCEPTION: That conservation of energy is an absolute, bedrock law [01:41]. The video opens with an astronaut throwing a rock in deep space; instead of coasting forever per Newton's first law, it eventually slows down and stops, and asks where the energy went [00:00-00:24] (on-screen at [00:10]: the rock with 'v = Constant' and an arrow, plus a clock). The punchline: energy as we usually think of it is NOT conserved in our expanding universe [16:55-17:00], because conservation laws only exist where there is a corresponding symmetry, and our universe lacks time symmetry over large timescales [16:44-16:48]. Einstein proposed a fix; unpaid mathematician Emmy Noether proved he was wrong and created a new paradigm underlying all of particle physics [00:35-00:51].

SEGMENTS (all 8 chapters):

1) What is symmetry? [00:00-04:25] - The rock puzzle [00:00-00:24]; 1915 Gottingen, Einstein's six lectures on his not-yet-final general relativity, struggling to show total energy is conserved [00:57-01:20]; Hilbert searches and finds only the Bianchi identities, which conserve energy only in a completely empty universe [01:51-02:06]; he turns to his new assistant Emmy Noether [02:14-02:17]; her backstory: barred as an official student at Erlangen ('would overthrow all academic order') [02:28-02:38], a 1903 semester at Gottingen where she learned geometry via symmetry [02:42-02:50]; symmetry explained with a triangle's 3 mirror axes + 3 rotations (6 symmetries) [02:59-03:41] (on-screen at [03:01]: the physical triangle demo) and continuous symmetry via a function whose derivative is unchanged when shifted by a constant [03:45-04:20] (on-screen at [03:53]: graph of f(x) shifted by amount 'a').

2) Emmy Noether and Einstein [04:25-07:33] - Noether becomes a leading symmetry expert, second woman in Germany with a math PhD [04:25-04:34] (on-screen at [04:35]: one of her German academic papers). Einstein proposes a new conservation equation: energy of matter + energy of gravitational field = constant [04:43-04:54]; Noether is convinced this is a fundamental mistake because it disregards GR's foundational principle [04:59-05:07]. Background: special relativity (1905) applied only to inertial frames [05:11-05:27]; Einstein's 1907 question about accelerated systems [05:45-05:52]; the 'happiest thought' - a falling window cleaner feels weightless [06:10-06:37] (on-screen at [06:28]: animated falling window cleaner); the equivalence principle (rocket at 9.8 m/s^2 = standing on Earth) [07:00-07:13], meaning a theory of accelerating frames is also a theory of gravity [07:14-07:25].

3) General Covariance [07:33-11:59] - Laws must have the same form in every frame: general covariance [07:34-07:37]; tensors and vectors: components change with the coordinate system but the object stays the same [07:40-08:43] (on-screen at [08:12]: vector diagram '3x̂ + 2ŷ' vs '2a + 1b' in two coordinate systems). Noether's finding: Einstein's equation contained a PSEUDOTENSOR, which doesn't transform properly, so gravitational energy visible in one frame disappears in another [08:46-09:08]. She asks whether general covariance and energy conservation are simply incompatible [09:24-09:36], notes covariance is itself a symmetry [09:39-09:48], and explores symmetries of an empty static universe: translation symmetry gives conservation of momentum [10:02-10:58] (on-screen at [10:48]: 'Translation Symmetry -> Conservation of Momentum', animation credited on-screen to ScienceClic); rotational symmetry gives conservation of angular momentum [11:03-11:43] (on-screen at [11:39]: 'Rotation Symmetry -> Conservation of Angular Momentum'); then time symmetry is teed up [11:44-11:55] (at [12:09] Derek is on camera spinning a disc on a string outdoors).

4) The Principle of Least Action [11:59-15:29] - Everything follows the path minimizing the action (integral of Lagrangian L = kinetic minus potential energy), governed by the Euler-Lagrange equations [12:02-12:29]. A guest presenter works the math on a whiteboard: shift time by epsilon, L' = L + epsilon(dL/dt) [12:35-13:09] (on-screen at [13:23]: whiteboard titled 'Time Symmetry' with the Euler-Lagrange equation); chain rule + Euler-Lagrange substitution + reverse product rule [13:27-14:14] (on-screen at [13:54] and [14:15]: full color-coded derivation of dL/dt = d/dt(∂L/∂v · v)); the conserved quantity works out to 1/2 mv^2 + V, the total energy [14:19-15:21] (on-screen at [15:07]: whiteboard step d/dt(∂L/∂v · v - L) = 0 with L = T - V), so time translation symmetry = energy conservation [15:21-15:24].

5) Noether's First Theorem [15:29-18:24] - Any continuous symmetry gives a conservation law: translation->momentum, rotation->angular momentum, time translation->energy [15:37-16:04] (on-screen at [15:59]: 'dp/dt = 0' with translation arrows; at [15:39]: portrait of young Noether). But our universe isn't a static empty one: 1920s galaxy redshift measurements showed expansion, 1990s supernova data showed accelerating expansion [16:16-16:43] (on-screen at [16:50]: Big Bang/expansion visualization), so no time symmetry over large timescales, so energy isn't conserved [16:44-17:00]. Example: a CMB-era photon emitted 380,000 years after the Big Bang arrives as a microwave, having lost 99.9% of its energy - 'Where did the energy go? Doesn't go anywhere. Energy's not conserved.' [17:07-17:27]. Same for the opening rock: it slows and stops in the expanding universe; the energy just disappears [17:30-17:41] (on-screen at [17:42]: the rock with an 'Energy' bar drained to near-empty). No law is violated without the symmetry [17:52-17:56]. Energy still seems conserved day to day because time symmetry effectively holds on short timescales; only over millions of years does the breakdown show [18:18 leads in].

6) The Continuity Equation [18:24-23:20] - Short-timescale symmetry means energy is conserved 'for all intents and purposes' [18:25-18:38]; over millions of years expansion breaks it [18:40-18:51]. In GR, curvature varies point to point, killing global symmetries [19:05-19:24], but local symmetries (general covariance) survive [19:27-19:52]. Noether's SECOND theorem: local symmetries give not proper conservation laws but a continuity equation [19:55-20:06], illustrated by water in a pipe [20:08-20:44] (on-screen at [20:18]: '∂ρ/∂t + ∂j/∂x = 0' labeled 'Change in Amount of Water'). For GR, patches of space-time are the pipe and energy the water: conserved in any small patch [20:48-21:09] (on-screen at [21:10]: the same equation relabeled 'Change in Amount of Energy' / 'Net Flow of Energy' over glowing space-time patches), but linking patches brings in curvature - 'cracks' through which energy leaks [21:10-21:28] (on-screen at [22:02]: '∂μT^μν + Γ^μ_αμ T^αν + Γ^ν_αμ T^μα = 0' with the Γ terms labeled 'Curvature'); lost energy gets attributed to the gravitational field as the universe stretches [22:07-22:23]. Her continuity equation turned out to be exactly the Bianchi identities Hilbert had dismissed, now proven to be the best you can do in GR [22:28-22:46]. One paper solved what eluded Hilbert and Einstein [22:50-22:58]; an interviewee calls her two theorems probably the most important theorems for 20th-century physics [23:01-23:06]. Gottingen later made her a professor with a small salary from 1923 [23:07-23:16].

7) Escape from Germany [23:20-24:49] - Hitler becomes chancellor 30 January 1933; Jewish academics banned; a former student reports her Jewish heritage and she is suspended [23:21-23:37] (on-screen at [23:30]: archival footage of marching Nazi stormtroopers). She keeps teaching from her kitchen, even welcoming an old student who arrives in a Nazi stormtrooper's brown shirt [23:40-24:11]. She escapes to a teaching position at Bryn Mawr, a women's college in America, teaching there until her death [24:12-24:22]. Einstein's NYT obituary: 'the most significant creative mathematical genius thus far produced since the higher education of women began' [24:25-24:33]. Her theorem changed physicists' whole state of mind toward symmetry [24:37-24:47].

8) The Standard Model - Higgs and Quarks [24:49-27:39] - Symmetry ideas applied to the quantum world: electron phase can be offset globally with no physical change - a gauge symmetry - which leads to conservation of electric charge [24:49-25:21] (on-screen at [24:59]: electron 'e-' with a phase arrow inside a rainbow color wheel, animation credited to ScienceClic). In the 1960s-70s Noether's insights led to quarks and the Higgs boson, explained where forces come from and the origin of mass; her two theorems are the closest we've come to a theory of everything, with a sequel video promised [25:25-25:53]. The video closes with a KiwiCo sponsorship segment (Rapid-Fire Disc Launcher crate, code Veritasium for 50% off) [26:26-27:31].

HOW MUCH ENERGY IS LOST: The video's example is a photon of visible light emitted 380,000 years after the Big Bang [17:07-17:08]. It travels through the universe unimpeded and arrives at our telescopes not as visible light but as a microwave [17:12-17:16], having lost 99.9% of its energy [17:19].

WHERE THE ENERGY GOES: The video's primary, emphatic answer is that it goes nowhere: 'Where did the energy go? Doesn't go anywhere. Energy's not conserved.' [17:23-17:27]. The energy 'doesn't really go anywhere; it just disappears' [17:40]. This is allowed because conservation laws only exist where there is a corresponding symmetry (Noether's first theorem), and over large timescales our expanding, accelerating universe is not symmetric in time [16:44-16:48], so energy as we usually think of it isn't conserved [16:55-17:00]; 'There's no reason for energy to be conserved anymore 'cause you don't have that symmetry' [17:02-17:04]. No law of physics is violated [17:52-17:56]. On short timescales, time symmetry effectively holds, so energy appears conserved for all intents and purposes; only over timescales of millions of years does the loss become noticeable [18:25-18:51].

RECONCILING THE TWO ACCOUNTS IN THE VIDEO: There is a second, complementary framing later. In the continuity-equation picture from Noether's second theorem, energy IS conserved within any small local patch of space-time [21:02-21:09], but when patches are linked, curvature terms enter the equation and energy can 'leak out' through the cracks between patches [21:10-21:28]. As the energy term decreases, the curvature terms increase [22:02-22:05], and the interviewee says the lost energy 'we now start attributing it to things like the gravitational field, which has changed 'cause the whole universe is stretched' [22:07-22:14]. These aren't contradictory: the primary claim ([17:23-17:27], [17:40]) is that there is no globally conserved energy that the photon's loss flows into; the later passage ([22:07-22:14]) describes the local bookkeeping in GR, where the deficit is attributed to curvature/gravitational-field terms rather than to any recoverable store of energy. The video's headline answer remains 'it doesn't go anywhere.'

On-screen content note: the frames provided for this question show the opening rock with 'v = Constant' [00:10] (the same energy-loss phenomenon as the photon, per [17:30-17:37]) and, at [27:29], only the KiwiCo sponsor overlay ('KiwiCo.com/Veritasium, Code: Veritasium, 50% Off First Crate' with a QR code); no on-screen figure for the 99.9% number itself appears in this question's frames - the figure comes from the narration at [17:19].

EINSTEIN'S MISTAKE: In 1915, while finalizing general relativity, Einstein couldn't show that total energy was conserved in his new theory [00:57-01:20]. Bothered by this, he proposed a new conservation equation stating that the energy of matter plus the energy of the gravitational field stays constant - its change over time and space is zero [04:41-04:54]. When Noether examined it, she saw a fundamental mistake: the equation disregards the foundational principle general relativity is built on [04:59-05:07], namely general covariance - the requirement that the laws of gravity take the same form in every frame of reference [07:27-07:37]. To satisfy covariance you must use tensors, objects that stay the same when you change coordinate systems [07:40-08:43]. But Einstein's proposed energy-conservation equation contained a PSEUDOTENSOR, which 'isn't quite a tensor': it does not remain the same quantity when transformed between frames, so the gravitational energy you observe in one frame completely disappears in another [08:46-09:08]. As the interviewee puts it, people were 'trying to stamp conservation of energy into relativity by bending the rules of mathematics' [09:13-09:22]. The video frames this as the mistake Noether proved wrong ('Einstein came up with a possible solution, but then a little-known unpaid mathematician named Emmy Noether proved he was wrong' [00:35-00:42]).

HOW NOETHER PROVED HIM WRONG: Knowing his fix couldn't work, she asked whether general covariance and energy conservation are simply incompatible, and if so, why [09:24-09:36]. Recognizing that general covariance is itself a kind of symmetry [09:39-09:48], she analyzed which symmetries the universe actually has, starting with an empty static universe [09:51-09:58]:

1) First theorem: every continuous symmetry yields a conservation law - translation symmetry gives conservation of momentum [10:43-10:51], rotational symmetry gives conservation of angular momentum [11:37-11:40], and time translation symmetry gives conservation of energy [15:29-15:31, 16:02-16:04], derived via the principle of least action (the conserved quantity from time-shift symmetry works out to 1/2 mv^2 + V, the total energy [15:08-15:24]). The corollary: where a symmetry is absent, its conservation law simply does not hold - our expanding universe is not time-symmetric over large timescales, so energy as we usually think of it isn't conserved [16:44-17:00], and that violates no laws [17:52-17:56]. So the global energy conservation Einstein was trying to force into GR shouldn't be expected in the first place.

2) Second theorem: GR's surviving symmetries are local ones (general covariance holds in any small region) [19:27-19:52], and for local symmetries you no longer get proper conservation laws at all - only a continuity equation that works locally [19:55-20:06]. Energy is conserved in any small patch of space-time [21:02-21:09], but linking patches brings in curvature terms, through which energy can effectively leak [21:10-21:28, 21:57-22:05]. Crucially, her continuity equation turned out to be exactly equivalent to the Bianchi identities - the 'half-solution' Hilbert had found and dismissed because it gave proper energy conservation only in an empty universe - and Noether proved this was the best you could do in general relativity [22:28-22:46]. With one paper she uncovered the source of all conservation laws and solved the problem that had eluded both Hilbert and Einstein [22:50-22:58].

In short: Einstein's error was forcing global energy conservation into GR with a frame-dependent pseudotensor, violating general covariance; Noether proved that conservation laws exist only where symmetries do, that our universe lacks the required global time symmetry, and that GR's local symmetry can give you at most a continuity equation (the Bianchi identities), never the proper global conservation law Einstein wanted.

On-screen content notes: at [04:35] the frame shows one of Noether's original German academic papers; at [08:12] (near the tensor explanation) an on-screen diagram shows the same vector written as '3x̂ + 2ŷ' in one coordinate system and '2a + 1b' in another - the visual basis for why a proper tensor is frame-independent while a pseudotensor is not; at [15:39] a portrait of the young Emmy Noether is shown; at [18:34] a ScienceClic-credited animation shows an astronaut with a clock, illustrating the symmetry arguments. No conflicting claims appear within this question's evidence.
